our_argument_summary: Our review says NMP deceleration in the Siberian sector is already explained by standard secular variation models (WMM2025, NOAA). WIN-059 is flagged as a duplicate of WIN-007. It is part of the WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 group where multiple WINs reuse the same NOAA pole position data. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-002 references NMP post-1990 acceleration -> WIN-007, mentions NP.xy dataset
raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W019: 'NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral (PENDING)' is the closest weekly test
SESSION-CONTEXT.md line 112 — Kill-Shot Test 6: Dome NMP drift rate currently FAILING at 39.9% error, exceeding own 30% threshold
build-scripts/build-doc-v4.js line 510 — Dashboard analysis: dome predicts 'exponential deceleration' with deceleration constant 0.08
monitor.py — not available in raw-text directory; WIN-007 curmudgeon review identified Domain #11 as NMP monitoring domain (fetches NOAA NP.xy, checks longitude > 130°E)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline specifically validates WIN-059's 'Siberian sector deceleration' claim. The dome's monitor.py Domain #11 (per WIN-007 review) fetches NOAA NP.xy data and checks only whether the current pole longitude exceeds 130°E. It performs no sector-specific deceleration analysis, no Siberian flux lobe tracking, and no velocity-vs-time computation. The dome's Kill-Shot test (Test 6) predicts drift rate via exponential extrapolation (deceleration constant 0.08), but this is a single global rate, not a Siberian-sector-specific deceleration.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The NMP monitoring domain (Domain #11) performs a live fetch of current pole position but only compares longitude to a hardcoded threshold (130°E). No deceleration computation exists. The Kill-Shot Test 6 dashboard reports the dome's NMP prediction is currently FAILING at 39.9% error — the predicted deceleration rate does not match observations. Neither the monitoring nor the dashboard validates Siberian-sector-specific deceleration.
data_sources_used: NOAA NP.xy (pole position time series — same source as WIN-006, WIN-007, WIN-036, WIN-043)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The claim specifies 'Siberian sector' without defining the geographic bounds, time window, or deceleration metric. This ambiguity allows retroactive redefinition of what constitutes the 'Siberian sector' and what 'deceleration' means. The dome's own Test 6 uses a single exponential deceleration constant (0.08) applied globally — if sector-specific behavior diverges from the global trend, the dome could claim success by switching between global and sector-specific framing as convenient.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make specific code claims about WIN-059 individually. The grouped detail_evidence correctly references WMM2025 and NOAA secular variation. No discrepancies between our claims and dome code infrastructure.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-059 claims Siberian-sector-specific NMP deceleration, but no monitoring domain in monitor.py validates sector-specific deceleration rates. Domain #11 (the NMP domain, per WIN-007 review) checks only current longitude > 130°E — it does not decompose drift by geographic sector, compute deceleration rates, or compare observed vs predicted deceleration in the Siberian flux lobe region. The word 'Siberian' does not appear in any monitoring code. WIN-059's claim is a text-level assertion with no automated validation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: NMP deceleration is well-documented in standard geophysics. The pole accelerated from ~10 km/yr (1970s) to ~55 km/yr (2005-2015), then began decelerating to ~40 km/yr by 2025. Livermore et al. (2020, Nature Geoscience) explain this as competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes on the core-mantle boundary — the Siberian lobe's dominance caused the acceleration, and changes in lobe strength are causing the deceleration. The dome renames this 'aetheric vortex threshold behavior' without changing any numerical prediction or providing an alternative mechanism for Siberian-sector-specific deceleration.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: NMP deceleration has been documented since approximately 2019. NOAA WMM2020 and WMM2025 both model the reduced drift rate. Chulliat et al. (2020, Earth, Planets and Space 72:27) analyze NMP speed variability. The dome model was created after these observations were published. WIN-059 adopts the known deceleration trend and labels it a 'confirmed prediction.' No evidence exists that the dome predicted Siberian-sector-specific deceleration before it was observed.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives Siberian-sector-specific deceleration from dome geometry. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) is axially symmetric — it predicts the same field at all azimuths for a given radius. It cannot predict sector-specific behavior (Siberian vs Canadian vs any other sector) because it has no azimuthal dependence. The dome's Kill-Shot Test 6 uses a single exponential deceleration constant (0.08) that is a curve-fit to observed data, not derived from dome geometric parameters. The 'Siberian sector' specificity is adopted from standard geophysics (Livermore 2020), not derived from dome equations.

holes_found.description: Our review calls WIN-059 a 'duplicate of WIN-007' but this is inaccurate. WIN-007 claims a post-1990 'phase transition' (acceleration) while WIN-059 claims the opposite phenomenon — deceleration. They describe different phases of the same trajectory. A dome defender could argue: 'WIN-007 predicted the acceleration phase; WIN-059 predicts the subsequent deceleration phase. These are temporally distinct phenomena that test different aspects of the model.' Our 'duplicate' label fails to engage with the claim that predicting both acceleration AND deceleration demonstrates model completeness.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'Duplicate of WIN-007' in the finding with 'Reuses WIN-007's NOAA dataset; dome's own NMP prediction currently failing at 39.9% error.' The stronger argument is not that it's a duplicate but that (1) the dome's actual NMP drift rate prediction is currently failing its own threshold (Test 6: 39.9% > 30%), (2) the 'Siberian sector' specificity cannot be derived from the axially symmetric dome geometry, and (3) standard geophysics (Livermore 2020, WMM2025) already models sector-specific flux lobe competition.

holes_found.description: We miss the most devastating counterargument against WIN-059: the dome's own Kill-Shot Test 6 shows its NMP drift rate prediction is FAILING at 39.9% error, exceeding its own 30% threshold. This is documented in SESSION-CONTEXT.md. WIN-059 claims the dome predicts Siberian-sector deceleration, but the dome's own dashboard shows it cannot even predict the global NMP drift rate accurately. Claiming sector-specific deceleration as a 'confirmed prediction' while the model's overall NMP prediction is failing is internally contradictory. Our review text does not mention this.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'The dome model's own Kill-Shot Test 6 reports NMP drift rate prediction currently failing at 39.9% error (exceeding the model's own 30% acceptability threshold). A model that cannot predict the NMP's global drift rate accurately cannot claim sector-specific deceleration as a confirmed prediction. The deceleration constant (0.08) used in Test 6 is a curve-fit extrapolation, not derived from dome geometry, and it demonstrably does not match current observations.'

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence is generic — it references WMM2025 and NOAA broadly but doesn't explain what 'NMP deceleration in Siberian sector' actually means as a physical claim, why the Siberian sector is relevant, or how standard geophysics explains sector-specific behavior. A dome defender reading our review would say we didn't engage with the actual claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Add substantive content to detail_evidence explaining: (1) The NMP's trajectory toward Siberia is driven by competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes on the core-mantle boundary (Livermore 2020). (2) The recent deceleration (~55 km/yr peak to ~40 km/yr by 2025) is attributed to changes in Siberian flux lobe intensity. (3) The dome's axially symmetric geometry H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) has no azimuthal dependence and cannot predict sector-specific behavior. (4) The dome's own Test 6 is failing at 39.9% error.

holes_found.description: The detail_claim is vague: 'NMP deceleration in Siberian sector follows dome model.' It doesn't specify what numerical prediction the dome makes for Siberian-sector deceleration, what the measured deceleration rate is, or how 'follows dome model' is defined. Our review doesn't note this vagueness.
holes_found.recommendation: Note in detail_evidence that the dome provides no specific numerical prediction for Siberian-sector deceleration rate, no defined geographic bounds for 'Siberian sector,' and no derivation from dome geometry. The claim is unfalsifiable as stated because it specifies no success criterion.

stronger_arguments.description: The axial symmetry argument (from WIN-043 review) applies with even greater force here. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance r from the disc center. It has NO azimuthal (angular) dependence. 'Siberian sector' is an azimuthal specification — it refers to a particular longitude range on Earth. An axially symmetric model fundamentally cannot predict that deceleration should occur preferentially in one azimuthal sector rather than another. For the dome to make Siberian-sector-specific predictions, it would need to break its own radial symmetry, which it has no mechanism to do. The 'Siberian sector' label is adopted from standard geophysics (where it refers to the Siberian flux lobe on the core-mantle boundary) and mapped onto the dome model without justification.
sources: Livermore et al. 2020, Nature Geoscience — competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes drives NMP trajectory
Dome site H(r) formula — axially symmetric, no azimuthal dependence

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's Test 6 is failing at 39.9% error — this is the single strongest argument against WIN-059. The dome claims to predict NMP behavior, yet its own quantitative test of NMP drift rate shows the prediction does not match observations. The deceleration constant (0.08) used in the dome's exponential extrapolation is a curve-fit parameter, not derived from dome geometry, and it demonstrably produces incorrect current predictions. Claiming a sector-specific deceleration WIN while the model's own global NMP test is failing is self-contradictory.
sources: SESSION-CONTEXT.md line 112 — Kill-Shot Test 6 failing at 39.9% error

stronger_arguments.description: WIN-059 is the eighth WIN to use NOAA NMP/pole position data (after WIN-006, 007, 036, 040, 041, 042, 043). Counting the same NMP trajectory eight different ways does not create eight independent confirmations. The data has one degree of freedom: the NMP's position over time. Extracting acceleration, deceleration, direction, sector-specificity, longitude deviation, and drift ratios from the same time series is redundant arithmetic that inflates the win count.
sources: NOAA NP.xy pole position time series
data/wins.json — detail_group 'WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063' showing 8 WINs in same cluster

kernel_of_truth.description: The NMP has indeed been decelerating since approximately 2019, dropping from a peak speed of ~55 km/yr to ~40 km/yr by 2025. This deceleration is associated with changes in the Siberian flux lobe on the core-mantle boundary — the dome model correctly identifies that the NMP trajectory is linked to a 'Siberian' direction and that the drift rate is changing. The basic observation of NMP deceleration is genuine and geophysically significant.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Four fatal problems: (1) The deceleration was documented in standard geophysics (Chulliat 2020, WMM2020/2025) before the dome claimed it as a prediction — this is post-hoc retrodiction. (2) The dome's own quantitative NMP prediction (Test 6) is currently failing at 39.9% error, demonstrating the model cannot accurately predict NMP behavior. (3) The dome's axially symmetric geometry has no mechanism for sector-specific predictions — 'Siberian' is borrowed from standard geophysics terminology for core-mantle boundary flux lobes, which the dome model does not model. (4) The claim provides no specific numerical prediction, geographic bounds, or success criterion, making it unfalsifiable as stated.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-059 claims that NMP deceleration in the 'Siberian sector' follows the dome model. This is one of the V51.0 additions (new_in_v51: true) and belongs to the large WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 cluster that reuses NOAA geomagnetic data across multiple claims.

**What the Claim Actually Is**

The North Magnetic Pole accelerated dramatically from ~10 km/yr in the 1970s to ~55 km/yr around 2005-2015, then began decelerating to ~40 km/yr by 2025. The trajectory has been predominantly toward Siberia (from the Canadian Arctic into the Eastern Hemisphere). 'Siberian sector deceleration' appears to mean that the pole's deceleration is associated with its approach toward (or interaction with) the Siberian flux lobe region. The dome model claims this behavior 'follows' its predictions.

**Critical Missing Information**

Our review text is thin. The detail_claim states only 'NMP deceleration in Siberian sector follows dome model' without specifying: (1) what numerical deceleration rate the dome predicts, (2) what geographic bounds define the 'Siberian sector,' (3) how 'follows dome model' is operationally defined, or (4) what would constitute a failed prediction. Our rebuttal (three sentences) references WMM2025 and NOAA generically but doesn't engage with the substance of the claim.

**The Test 6 Failing Problem — Our Biggest Missed Argument**

SESSION-CONTEXT.md documents that the dome's own Kill-Shot Test 6 (NMP drift rate) is currently FAILING at 39.9% error, exceeding the dome's own 30% acceptability threshold. The dome uses an exponential deceleration model with constant 0.08 (a curve-fit, not geometry-derived). This constant demonstrably produces predictions that don't match current observations. Claiming WIN-059 as a 'confirmed prediction' while Test 6 simultaneously shows the model's NMP prediction is failing is self-contradictory. Our review text for WIN-059 does not mention this. This is a major gap.

**The Axial Symmetry Problem**

The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) is radially symmetric — it depends only on radial distance from the disc center and has no azimuthal (angular) dependence. 'Siberian sector' is inherently an azimuthal specification (a particular longitude range). An axially symmetric model cannot predict sector-specific geomagnetic behavior. For standard geophysics, 'Siberian flux lobe' refers to a specific feature on the core-mantle boundary — a region of enhanced magnetic flux in the Siberian/Central Asian sector that competes with the Canadian flux lobe to determine NMP trajectory (Livermore et al. 2020). The dome model has no core, no mantle boundary, and no mechanism for azimuthally non-uniform flux features. The 'Siberian sector' label is borrowed from standard geophysics terminology without any dome-geometric basis.

**Duplicate vs Different Claim**

Our review calls WIN-059 a 'duplicate of WIN-007,' but this is imprecise and potentially counterproductive. WIN-007 claims a post-1990 acceleration (phase transition). WIN-059 claims the opposite — deceleration. These describe temporally distinct phases of the same trajectory. A dome defender would argue these are independent predictions: one about acceleration onset (~1990), another about deceleration onset (~2019). The correct framing is not 'duplicate' but: (a) both use the same NOAA NP.xy dataset, (b) neither is derived from dome geometry, (c) the dome's own NMP prediction is currently failing, and (d) 'Siberian sector' specificity is borrowed from standard geophysics.

**Data Reuse Pattern**

WIN-059 is approximately the eighth WIN to use NOAA pole position data (WIN-006, 007, 036, 040, 041, 042, 043, 059). The entire WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 group represents data slicing: taking well-known geophysical observations (NMP trajectory, SAA evolution, magnetic field decay) and splitting them into many 'predictions' to inflate the win count. WIN-059 adds 'Siberian-sector deceleration' as yet another slice of the same trajectory.

**Verdict Assessment**

'Std Model Explains' is correct. Standard geophysics (Livermore 2020, Chulliat 2020, WMM2025) fully explains NMP deceleration as a consequence of core-mantle boundary flux lobe dynamics. The dome adds no explanatory power. However, the current text needs significant strengthening: adding the Test 6 failure (39.9% error), the axial symmetry argument, and the lack of any specific numerical prediction.

**Recommendations**

1. Rewrite finding: Replace 'Duplicate of WIN-007; dome has no mechanism to predict sector-specific deceleration' with 'Dome's own NMP prediction (Test 6) failing at 39.9% error; axially symmetric geometry cannot derive sector-specific deceleration'
2. Expand detail_evidence substantially: Add Test 6 failure, axial symmetry argument, Livermore 2020 reference, and data reuse pattern
3. Update detail_verdict_text: Include the self-contradictory nature of claiming NMP predictions while the model's own NMP test is failing
4. Consider whether verdict should be 'Misleading' rather than 'Std Model Explains' given the data reuse and the dome's own test failing
